name: code-refactoring-refactor-clean
description: You are a code refactoring expert specializing in clean code principles, SOLID design patterns, and modern software engineering best practices. Analyze and refactor the provided code to improve its quality, maintainability, and performance.
capabilities: Analyze code for code smells, SOLID violations, and performance issues
Generate prioritized refactoring plans based on ROI analysis
Apply design patterns (Factory, Strategy, Observer, Repository, Decorator)
Decompose monolithic code into modular, testable components
Implement SOLID principles with concrete before/after examples
Create comprehensive test suites for refactored code
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 90 static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns appear in markdown documentation files as educational code examples demonstrating refactoring patterns. The skill contains no executable code - only AI prompts and documentation. All patterns (backtick execution, email operations, file access, weak crypto references, reconnaissance commands) are within code blocks in implementation-playbook.md as teaching examples for proper refactoring. No security risks exist.
valueStatement: Transform tangled, hard-to-maintain code into clean, modular architecture using proven refactoring patterns and SOLID design principles. This skill provides expert guidance on identifying code smells, applying design patterns, and implementing incremental improvements that enhance code quality without breaking functionality.
userTitle: Refactor and Clean Code with SOLID Principles
limitations: Cannot execute automated refactoring without user approval
Requires human review to validate business logic preservation
May not understand project-specific domain constraints without context
Cannot guarantee performance improvements without benchmarking

useCases.title: Legacy Code Modernization
useCases.description: Transform legacy monolithic applications into clean, modular architecture following SOLID principles and modern design patterns

useCases.title: Code Review Improvements
useCases.description: Identify and fix code smells, reduce complexity, and improve maintainability during pull request reviews

useCases.title: Onboarding Best Practices
useCases.description: Learn refactoring patterns and clean code principles through practical examples and step-by-step migration guides

promptTemplates.title: Simple Refactoring Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor this code to improve readability and reduce duplication
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick code quality improvements for small functions

promptTemplates.title: Comprehensive Code Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this code for code smells, SOLID violations, and technical debt. Provide a prioritized refactoring plan.
promptTemplates.scenario: Deep analysis of larger codebases or modules

promptTemplates.title: Design Pattern Application
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply appropriate design patterns to refactor this code following SOLID principles. Show before/after comparison.

promptTemplates.title: Full Refactoring with Tests
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor this code completely including test suite generation, migration guide, and metrics report showing improvements

bestPractices: Always run existing tests before and after refactoring to ensure behavior preservation
Apply refactoring in small, incremental steps rather than large-scale rewrites
Use the implementation-playbook.md reference for detailed patterns and examples
antiPatterns: Do not refactor without tests - always establish test coverage first
Avoid over-engineering simple solutions - YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)
Do not change external behavior without explicit stakeholder approval

faq.question: Will refactoring break my existing code?
faq.answer: No, when done correctly. This skill emphasizes preserving external behavior while improving internal structure. Always run tests before and after refactoring to validate behavior remains unchanged.

faq.question: How do I know if code needs refactoring?
faq.answer: Look for code smells: long methods (>20 lines), large classes (>200 lines), high cyclomatic complexity (>10), duplicate code, poor naming, or difficulty testing. The skill can analyze your code and identify these issues automatically.

faq.question: Can this skill automatically refactor my code?
faq.answer: The skill provides refactoring guidance and generates refactored code examples, but you should review and apply changes manually. Automated refactoring requires human oversight to validate business logic and domain-specific constraints.

faq.question: What are SOLID principles?
faq.answer: SOLID is an acronym for five design principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion. These principles help create maintainable, flexible, and scalable software architecture.

faq.question: Should I refactor all code at once?
faq.answer: No. Refactor incrementally using the Boy Scout Rule: leave the code cleaner than you found it. Prioritize high-impact, low-effort changes first. The skill provides ROI analysis to help prioritize refactoring efforts.

faq.question: How do I convince my team to refactor?
faq.answer: Focus on business value: reduced bugs, faster feature development, easier onboarding, and lower maintenance costs. Use the skill's metrics report to show before/after comparisons demonstrating concrete improvements in code quality and testability.
